US Rental Investment Markets

Zillow data as of: February 28, 2026

Where should you buy rental property in the US? This page ranks 3803 cities by investment metrics derived from Zillow’s public home value and rent indices plus Census demand signals – and a few unconventional ones like Trader Joe’s adjacency, a classic real-estate proxy for walkable, amenity-rich neighborhoods with strong demographics.

Jump to: Glossary · Cash Flow Calculator · Trader Joe’s Adjacency · Unicorn Leaderboard · TJ Map · Listing Activity · Rent vs FMR Reality Check · Job Market · Hottest Counties · ZIP Drill-Down · Leveraged Returns · Hidden Gems · Composite Score

The price-to-rent ratio is the standard cash-flow benchmark:

  • Strong Buy (P/R < 15): Cash flow positive from day one
  • Moderate (P/R 15–20): Reasonable returns, some appreciation upside
  • Lean Rent (P/R 20–25): Marginal; depends on appreciation
  • Rent Favored (P/R > 25): Buy only if you’re betting on price growth

Plain-English Glossary

Every term used on this page, translated. If you only read one section, read this one.

Plain-English Glossary
Every metric and acronym on this page, in normal words
Term What it means
Prices & rents
ZHVI (home value) Zillow's estimate of the typical home price in a city. Think "middle of the market" -- not the cheapest, not a mansion.
ZORI (rent index) Zillow's estimate of the typical asking rent for a new lease in a city. Similar to a Zestimate but for rent.
YoY (year-over-year) How much a number has changed vs the same month one year ago. +5% YoY = 5% higher than last year.
Price-to-rent ratio (P/R) Home price ÷ annual rent. Low P/R (under 15) means rent is high relative to the price -- good for landlords. High P/R (over 25) means the home is expensive relative to the rent it earns.
Returns
Gross yield Annual rent ÷ home price, as a percent. Ignores costs and financing. Quick first-pass return number.
Cap rate Gross yield after subtracting 35% for expected operating expenses (taxes, insurance, maintenance, vacancy, property management). On this page: cap rate = gross yield × 0.65.
Operating expenses (opex) Ongoing costs of owning the rental: property taxes, insurance, repairs, vacancy losses, property management. Rule of thumb: ~35% of rent.
Leverage
Leverage Using borrowed money (a mortgage) to buy the property. Amplifies returns both up and down.
PITI Principal + Interest + Taxes + Insurance. The four pieces of a typical monthly mortgage payment.
Monthly cash flow Rent received minus PITI minus operating expenses, per month. Positive = the tenant covers the mortgage and you pocket the difference.
Cash-on-cash return (CoC) Annual cash flow ÷ the cash you actually put in (down payment + closing costs). Tells you what your own money is earning.
1% rule / 2% rule Quick sanity checks. 1% rule: monthly rent is at least 1% of the purchase price. 2% is twice that bar -- rare, mostly distressed markets.
Yield spread Gross yield minus the current mortgage rate. Positive spread = rent covers the loan with room to spare. Negative = you're subsidizing the mortgage and betting on price growth.
Demand
Vacancy rate Share of rental units sitting empty. High vacancy = harder to find tenants and more rent concessions. Under 10% is generally healthy.
Net migration People moving in minus people moving out. Positive = growing demand; negative = shrinking.
Population growth Total population change over a period, as a percent. Includes births, deaths, and migration.
Unemployment rate (BLS LAUS) Share of the local workforce that's unemployed. Low + falling = healthy job market = tenants who can pay rent.
Listing health
DOM (days on market) Median days it takes a listing to go under contract. Low DOM = hot market; high DOM = stale.
Sale-to-list ratio Final sale price ÷ original list price. Above 100% = bidding wars; below 100% = buyers have leverage.
Price drops Share of listings that had at least one price reduction. High = sellers are chasing the market down.
Hotness score (Realtor.com) Realtor.com's 0--100 composite ranking demand vs supply. Higher = more competitive for buyers.
Benchmarks
HUD FMR (Fair Market Rent) The government's official rent benchmark per bedroom count per metro/county. Used to set housing voucher caps; we use it to flag cities where market rent (ZORI) looks off.
SAFMR Small-Area FMR -- same concept as FMR but at the ZIP level. Lets you spot neighborhood-level outliers.
Proximity
Trader Joe's adjacency Distance (in miles) from the city center to the nearest Trader Joe's. Used as a quick proxy for walkable, amenity-rich neighborhoods with strong demographics -- places where demand tends to be durable.
Unicorn A market that clears multiple bars at once -- positive cash flow, strong yield, and close to a Trader Joe's. Rare on purpose.
Composite
Composite investment score A 0--100 rank-based score blending cap rate, population growth, net migration, vacancy, YoY price appreciation, and the new listing-market and job-market signals. Each factor is converted to a percentile so one extreme number can't dominate.
Tier (Strong Buy / Moderate / Lean Rent / Rent Favored) Bucket assigned by price-to-rent ratio. Strong Buy = cheap relative to rent; Rent Favored = expensive, only works if prices keep rising.

Data Sources

All data is traceable and reproducible. Run Rscript scripts/us_rental_markets_data.R to refresh.

Data Sources
All public, no API key required
Source Description Updated
Zillow ZHVI Typical home values (city level, SFR+condo, 35th–65th percentile) 2026-02-28
Zillow ZORI Typical observed rents (city level, SFR+condo+MFR) 2026-02-28
Zillow ZIP ZHVI+ZORI Same indices at ZIP granularity for neighborhood drill-down 2026-02-28
Census Pop Estimates City + county population (2020–2024), net migration V2024
Census ACS 5-Year Vacancy rates, renter %, housing counts (county level) 2019–2023
Census Places + ZCTA Gazetteer Lat/lon for every incorporated place + ZIP (distance calcs) 2024
Redfin Data Center Listing activity: DOM, price drops, sale-to-list, new listings (county) Monthly
HUD Fair Market Rents (FY26) Government rent benchmark by bedroom count; flags ZORI outliers FY2026
BLS LAUS Monthly metro + county unemployment rate and 12-month change Latest month
Realtor.com RDC Active/new/pending listings, hotness score, median DOM (county + ZIP) Monthly
Trader Joe’s Store Locator All 464 US stores, scraped from locations.traderjoes.com 2026
Cap rate = gross yield × (1 − 35% expense ratio). Raw data: us_rental_markets.csv · us_rental_markets_zip.csv

Interactive Cash Flow Calculator

Plug in your financing assumptions and rerank every market by monthly cash flow, cash-on-cash return, and whether it actually clears the mortgage. Defaults are 25% down, 7.25% 30-yr fixed, 35% operating expenses, 3% closing costs.

# City State Price Rent/mo Mortgage Cash flow/mo CoC % Cap rate Pop TJ mi Vacancy Tier

The Trader Joe’s Adjacency Premium

Real-estate folks have long used Trader Joe’s as a proxy for walkable, amenity-rich, demographically-solid neighborhoods. Every one of the 462 TJ store cities on this page lives near one of 464 US Trader Joe’s, scraped fresh from the official store locator.

Here’s how TJ-adjacent cities (within 10 miles of a store) compare to everyone else on the metrics investors care about:

The premium is real: cities within 2 miles of a TJ carry substantially higher prices and rents, with stronger YoY growth, than far-flung markets. That’s not surprising – TJ picks its own locations carefully, favoring areas with median income, education, and density profiles that are already strong.

The practical implication for an investor: proximity to a TJ is a useful check on whether a cash-flow market is also demographically durable. Cheap cap rates in dying small towns are a trap; cheap cap rates in neighborhoods near a Trader Joe’s are usually worth a closer look.

Unicorn Leaderboard: Cash Flow + Trader Joe’s

The holy grail: a rental market that’s cash-flow positive at 25% down and 7.25% rates AND within 10 miles of a Trader Joe’s. These are rare because TJ tends to pick areas where home prices have already outrun rents. But they exist.

## **62unicorn cities** found across 15 states, 19 metros.

Trader Joe’s Investment Map

Every US Trader Joe’s (purple dots) with the top rental-investment cities overlaid, colored by composite score. Click a marker for the full stats. The map loads live tiles, so it’ll only render online.

Listing Activity: How Fast Are Homes Moving?

ZHVI and ZORI are index values — smoothed, interpolated estimates of what a typical home is worth. But actual buyers face an actual market: listings with actual days-on-market, actual bidding activity, actual price drops. Redfin’s Data Center publishes county-level statistics for every listed home in the US. That tells us whether a given market is hot or cold independent of the index level.

Summary by days-on-market bucket
DOM bucket Cities Median cap % Sale / list % Sold above list % With price drops %
<= 15 days 136 2.40 100.3 41.3 27.7
16-30 days 419 3.10 100.3 41.6 25.2
31-45 days 911 3.42 99.5 36.3 23.6
46-60 days 753 3.73 98.6 23.5 25.1
61-90 days 851 4.03 97.7 14.4 27.5
> 90 days 437 3.94 96.9 11.3 24.7

Reading this: higher DOM bucket = slower market = more price drops, fewer bids over ask. The tradeoff for the investor is that slower markets also tend to have higher cap rates — because cash flow is what saves you when there’s no easy flip. Fast markets are often appreciation plays where you’re buying momentum.

Hot markets: cash-flow-positive AND moving fast

These meet three conditions simultaneously: cash-flow positive at our default assumptions, median DOM <= 25 days, and sale-to-list ratio >= 1.0 (bidding wars). That’s rare — most hot markets are overpriced. When they coexist, it’s a signal worth a second look.

## No cities currently qualify.

Rent vs HUD FMR Reality Check

HUD Fair Market Rents are the government-published benchmark rent used for Section 8 vouchers and affordable-housing programs. HUD sets them at roughly the 40th percentile of standard-quality rentals in each county. That makes them a useful ground-truth check on Zillow’s ZORI: if the index disagrees with HUD by a huge margin, it’s often either a data glitch or a signal that your city has a genuine luxury market (think: Napa, Aspen, parts of the Hamptons).

Most cities cluster slightly above the parity line — ZORI’s typical-rent is higher than HUD’s 40th-percentile FMR, as expected. Cities that shoot above the 2x FMR line are either (a) legitimately upscale rental markets or (b) index glitches.

Top FMR Outliers

When ZORI is below FMR (undervalued rental markets)

If Zillow’s ZORI is meaningfully below the HUD 40th-percentile benchmark, that’s either a new listing with room to raise rent or a market where tenants are under-paying for the property quality. Either way it’s an interesting signal.

Job Market vs Rental Yield

Rental demand ultimately depends on jobs — tenants need income to pay rent. BLS Local Area Unemployment Statistics publishes monthly unemployment rates for every US metro and county. Low unemployment = strong local economy = reliable tenant base, but usually also = expensive real estate. The sweet spot is a market with below-average unemployment and above-average cap rate.

Sweet-spot: low unemployment AND above-median cap rate

Hottest Markets (Realtor.com)

Realtor.com’s hotness score is a composite of supply (how quickly listings sell) and demand (how many unique shoppers view each listing). It’s computed at the county level, monthly, and ranks every US county from 1 (hottest) to ~1,600. Independent from price — a hot market can be cheap. Combined with our composite score, it surfaces counties where listing velocity agrees with the fundamentals.

ZIP-Level Neighborhood Drill-Down

City-level Zillow data averages the whole city — great for comparing Austin to Nashville, useless for comparing East Austin to West Austin. This drill-down uses Zillow’s ZIP-level ZHVI + ZORI (~8,100 ZIPs with both), re-runs the same leverage math, and attaches each ZIP’s nearest Trader Joe’s distance.

Tightest clusters: ZIPs with both TJ access AND cash flow

ZIPs within 5 miles of a Trader Joe’s and cash-flow positive — the ZIP-level version of the “unicorn” leaderboard.

Gross Yield vs Home Value

Leveraged Returns (at 25% Down, 7.25% 30yr)

Cap rate ignores financing. For the leveraged buyer the two numbers that matter are monthly cash flow (does the tenant cover the mortgage?) and cash-on-cash return (what does my out-of-pocket cash actually earn?). Both are computed at the default assumptions printed above – use the calculator for your own numbers.

Demand Health Check: Cap Rate vs Population Growth

High cap rate means nothing if the town is emptying out. This chart cross-references cap rate with population growth – the sweet spot is the upper-right quadrant: strong cash flow and growing population.

Hidden Gems: Small Towns with Big Cash Flow

A common investor strategy is to buy in smaller markets where price is low but rent holds up. The Hidden Gem flag filters for:

  • Population 5K–50K
  • Estimated cap rate ≥ 5%
  • Non-negative population growth
  • County vacancy rate ≤ 12%
## ** 91 cities** match the Hidden Gem criteria.
Show hidden gems

Investor Rules of Thumb

Two rent-to-price benchmarks investors use as a quick smell test:

  • 1% rule: Monthly rent ≥ 1% of purchase price. A pass usually means cash flow is plausible.
  • 2% rule: Monthly rent ≥ 2% of purchase price. Rare; typically only in distressed or low-priced markets.

Vacancy Rate vs Cap Rate

Markets with high vacancy rates are risky – your rental sits empty longer. Cities in the lower-right have strong cap rates and low vacancy.

Full US Market Explorer

Search, filter, and sort all 3803 cities.

Show full table

Composite Investment Score

A rank-based composite (0–100) that blends the signals investors actually care about: cap rate (35%), population growth (20%), net migration (15%), low vacancy (15%), and YoY price appreciation (15%). Each factor is converted to a percentile so outliers can’t dominate.

Top 50 by composite score

State Leaderboard

Best cash-flow city per state (highest monthly cash flow at default assumptions), plus state-level medians.

Yield Minus Mortgage Rate Spread

Gross rental yield minus the current mortgage rate (7.25%). Positive spread means the rent covers debt service with room to spare; negative means you’re subsidizing the mortgage and relying on appreciation.

Price vs Rent Growth (YoY)

Home value growth and rent growth tell different stories. When home prices outrun rents, cap rates compress (bad for investors); when rents outrun prices, cap rates expand.

Per-City Detail Lookup

Search for any city to see the full investor profile in one place.

Metro-Level Comparison

Average metrics across cities within each metro area (metros with 5+ cities).

Show metro table

How to update this data: Run Rscript scripts/us_rental_markets_data.R to download Zillow ZHVI + ZORI. Then run Rscript scripts/us_rental_demand_data.R to merge Census population / vacancy / migration and compute leverage metrics (mortgage payment, cash flow, cash-on-cash, composite score). Then re-render this page.
